First of all, what is your role? Well, your role as a consultant coach is not to be of service to clients. Being of service to clients implies you are a servant to clients. That's not what we do. Our role is actually to control clients. And to attempt to get compliance from clients. I often tell the story of my experience as a patient with chiropractors. And so the very first chiropractor I went to, I'm pretty severe back trouble at the time, I go in and how many of you have been to a chiro? Okay, you guys all know the drill, right? They bring in, they show you the x-rays and they do the report of findings and they tell you you've got to come in four times a week for the first three weeks and three times a week for the next six weeks, etc. And I know the drill too. I knew it when I went in. So I had sort of mentally prepared. So the doc gets down with the whole presentation, that's all fine except, you know, that's fine for ordinary people but that's no good for me. Because, you know, I got a bunch of stuff going on. And sometimes I got to jump on an airplane with a mom's notice and I can't be scheduled on appointments a week ahead of time. And you know, it's all the way, we all get done. We've arrived in an arrangement that I'll come in when I want to and I'll call them up a couple hours and they'll squeeze me in. And how long do you think I kept going? Not very damn long. A few months later I now go to my second chiropractor because of course my back problem is worse. And she does pretty much the same presentation. I do pretty much the same presentation. She now concludes the presentation by, excuse me, walking out, coming back in with the yellow pages, opening it to chiropractors and have a red pen in her hand and saying, let me mark three or four doctors I'd be happy to refer you to who will accept you on that basis and are very good doctors here in the community but you can't be a patient here at this practice on that basis. Huh? Well, because we have a relationship here, we're in a partnership to get you well. I can't do my part if you won't do your part and I don't want any patients here who aren't going to get well so I'm not going to accept you as a patient. Well, wait a minute, maybe I can arrange my schedule. When I went back for my first adjustment, all chiropractors give you some kind of little Xerox piece of paper with little drawings of exercises on it that you're supposed to do and they mark the number of reps on the blank line, right? You're supposed to do eight of these, four of these, right? Which immediately goes under the car seat is where that goes. And so I go back to my first adjustment and Dr. Pence says, how are you doing with those three exercises? I'll grant you fabulous. Like, I'm good at doing these exercises, right? Get out of here. Well, show me exercise number three. I want to make sure you're doing it right. You got to fess up, right? I'm not going to do it. I don't even know what exercise three is. The sheets under the car seat. And I haven't got any intention to do it. Just crack my back, crack my back and my neck. Let me get out of here. You know, I've got things to do. Now, you've got to go home. You can't be a patient here if you're not going to do your exercises. So we'll reschedule you for Thursday and come back in and be prepared to show me all three exercises before you get an adjustment. Well, you know, I'm stopping and pissed off, but I go do the exercises, you know, come back, do the exercise. So, seeing most doctors would think that their role is to be of service to the patient. But no, the role is to be the superior being in control in order to get compliance from the patient. So if you have an in-service attitude, the relationship is ordered in the wrong direction. If you have an in-service attitude, you give up control to the client. Second attitude, superiority. Nothing is more important to the successful consultant coach than complete disdain for everybody you're dealing with. That doesn't mean you don't like them, but you've got to really have in your head how incredibly ignorant and dysfunctional they all are. And I used to think, somebody asked me this question the other day, how much money do you have to take from somebody to get a smart person who can follow instructions? And I said, I used to think it was about that, you know, okay, we're only taking 30,000 from them and they're behaving like idiots. Maybe if we took 300,000 from them, they'd take it more seriously and they'd behave better. No.
